summary:
The Norman Conquest of England (1066 AD) was one of the most transformative events in English history, replacing the Anglo-Saxon ruling class with a French-speaking Norman nobility after [[event:3532|William the Conqueror's]] victory at the [[event:2234|Battle of Hastings]], and fundamentally reshaping English language, culture, law, and governance in ways that persist to the present day. The conquest followed the death of King Edward the Confessor without a clear heir, triggering competing claims from Harold Godwinson (the powerful English earl who was crowned immediately), William Duke of Normandy (who claimed Edward had promised him the throne), and Harald Hardrada of Norway.

William's Norman army crossed the Channel and defeated and killed Harold at Hastings, then marched on London, where the remaining English leadership submitted. William was crowned at Westminster Abbey on Christmas Day, 1066. The conquest that followed was systematic and brutal: Norman lords replaced Anglo-Saxon thegns across England, castle-building imposed military control over a resentful population, and the great [[event:3533|Domesday Book]] survey of 1086 catalogued every asset in England with unprecedented administrative thoroughness. The Norman ruling class spoke French for generations (the English language absorbed thousands of French words in consequence, creating the Anglo-Norman hybrid that evolved into modern English), operated under a Norman feudal system, and connected England permanently to continental European politics. The conquest ended England's Scandinavian orientation — the Viking cultural sphere it had inhabited since the [[event:2143|Lindisfarne]] era — and integrated it into the Norman-French world that shaped medieval English civilization.
